Protected ammunition compartment.
If the bulkhead of this compartment is not penetrated when the armor is breached,
it provides full protection against propellant fires.
If the ammunition load includes explosive-filled projectiles (HE, HEAT, HESH),
it may provide protection in the event of detonation of a small number of such rounds.

External or protected fuel tanks.
These tanks are located outside the vehicle’s under-armor volume,
or are protected by separate armor elements.
If they are hit and catch fire, the fire does not damage the tank’s internal modules
or the crew and burns out on its own after some time.

Historical – uses a real or previously existing camouflage applied to a similar vehicle or platform. Camouflages of this type are visible to all players regardless of custom content display settings.

Semi-historical – based on a real camouflage pattern, but some details or the vehicle it is applied to may differ from the original. Camouflages of this type are visible to all players regardless of custom content display settings.
Fictional – a product of the author's imagination and may not have a real-world prototype. Camouflages of this type may be hidden from view by other players in the game settings.

Temporary Disabling Sound Modifications

We continue to actively develop the game’s audio, adding new features and improving sound quality. Due to these changes, some sound modifications have become incompatible with the current client version, leading to an increase in errors and crashes. To ensure a predictable and stable experience for all players, we’ve temporarily disabled support for sound modifications in War Thunder.

We value the community and mod authors and intend to maintain the ability to create and use sound modifications as an important part of the game. We have a publicly available official sound project for modding, and we are already working on:

 - A mechanism for evaluating the compatibility and functionality of sound modifications
 - A separate guide for creating sound modifications for the current version and structure of the FMOD audio project: bank sets, routing, parameters/events, etc. This guide will help you improve the compatibility of your modifications with the game, making them more stable and less likely to impact game performance.

As soon as these tools are ready, support for sound modifications will be re-enabled. Thank you for your understanding and support

Armor steel with high hardness and strength
Combined armor with high hardness and strength
Armor aluminum with high hardness and strength
Without armor and without high hardness and strength

Aramid fabric or ultra-high-molecular-weight polyethylene panels installed in the crew compartments of armored vehicles reduce the angle and velocity of fragments, significantly improving crew and equipment protection from small fragments generated during armor penetration.

Panels made of polyethylene and polyisobutylene are installed inside and outside the tank. Their primary purpose is to reduce the impact on the crew during a nuclear explosion, but in some cases they can also protect the crew and equipment from small fragments generated during armor penetration by reducing their velocity and angle of flight, although this is less effective than a specialized spall liner.

Armor protection is made using composite armor, which can consist of multiple layers of dissimilar materials, such as textolite, ceramic elements, NERA layers, and other armor materials. This approach provides a lighter weight than standard steel armor while maintaining the same resistance. For main battle tanks, composite armor is typically used in the most heavily protected frontal areas of the hull and turret.

Armor protection is manufactured partially or entirely using aluminum alloys. Aluminum armor provides a reduction in armor weight compared to homogeneous steel armor of the same resistance. The armor resistance of aluminum depends on the manufacturing technology and alloying elements used in its production.

Armor protection is made using steel armor plates. The projectile resistance of armor steel depends on the manufacturing technology. Rolled armor has higher armor resistance than cast armor due to the uniform distribution of defects during the rolling process. Certain types of steel, such as electroslag remelted steel and high-hardness steel, offer greater resistance than conventional steel armor.
There is no armor protection, the vehicle is made of non-armored materials and is not designed to be under enemy fire.
Bulletproof armor is designed to protect a combat vehicle from small arms fire and shell fragments.

Projectile-proof armor is designed to protect a combat vehicle from tank and anti-tank gunfire. For medium and main battle tanks, this typically means providing projectile-proof protection from the front, while the sides and rear are often significantly less protected.

Projectile-proof armor is designed to protect a combat vehicle from tank and anti-tank gunfire. For heavy tanks, this typically means the ability to withstand fire from more powerful guns than medium tanks, or over a wider range of frontal arc.
Projectile-proof armor is designed to protect a combat vehicle from tank and anti-tank gunfire.
Spall armor is designed to protect a combat vehicle from shell and rocket fragments in the event of an indirect hit.

Partial or full armor protection of gun mounts. Machine guns and autocannons may have fragmentation-resistant shields. Main and secondary calibre artillery may have open-type or enclosed-type turrets. Open armor implies protection from several sides; as a rule, from the front and the sides. Enclosed-type turrets are fully protected with armor steel.

A conventional designation for a ship’s armor protection scheme in which the thickest armor forms an enclosed volume that includes the most important compartments and machinery over a large part of the hull. All engine and boiler rooms, ammunition magazines, and command spaces are located within a single armored box formed by the belt armor and longitudinal bulkheads on the sides, the armored traverses (vertical transverse bulkheads protecting against shells and fragments travelling along the ship) at the bow and stern, and the armored deck above. The citadel is characteristic of battleships and battlecruisers

Presence of an anti-torpedo bulge — an external hull structure in the underwater portion, representing an additional cavity covering the midsection of the ship. The purpose of anti-torpedo bulges is to ensure that a torpedo detonates as far from the main hull as possible, while the force of the explosion and the hydrodynamic shock is dispersed to the greatest extent within the volume of the bulge itself, causing minimal damage to the ship’s hull, compartments, and machinery inside it. Bulges may have different internal designs and may be filled with water, fuel, or left empty. Their effectiveness against explosions of varying power depends on this internal structure — the more advanced a ship’s anti-torpedo protection is, the higher the explosive power the hull can withstand without sustaining critical damage

Armor protection of the ship’s side in the most important areas. Armor plates, as a rule, cover the machinery and boiler rooms in the central part of the hull. However, they may also be applied in other sections, protecting ammunition hoists, magazines, and various other compartments

Armor protection of the ship’s side along its entire length. Armor plates cover the hull side from bow to stern and may vary in thickness and height. As a rule, the thickest armor covers the central part of the ship in the area of the boiler and machinery rooms
Local use of armor plates to cover certain sections of the boat’s hull and superstructure: the engines, the wheelhouse, the bulwarks, or the shields
The use of armor plates to protect entire volumes of the hull or individual compartments. It may form an enclosed armored volume composed of the belt armor, the armored deck, and the traverses

Protection with armor steel not only of individual sections or compartments of the ship, but also of entire parts: anti-torpedo longitudinal bulkheads along the central part of the ship; armored decks covering ammunition magazines, machinery, and boiler rooms, or entire sections of the bow, midship, or stern. Alternatively, the deck may be armored from bow to stern in its entirety; vertical armored bulkheads protecting various compartments from fragments

Protection with armor steel of individual sections or compartments of the ship of increased importance: ammunition magazines, machinery and boiler rooms, steering engines, command and rangefinder posts, wheelhouses, combat and radio rooms, central artillery stations. It may be implemented as vertical bulkheads, horizontal plates, or entire armored decks. Additionally, groups of anti-aircraft guns or gun groups (clusters of guns of the same calibre located in one compartment) may be protected with splinter shields or bulwarks
Sustained turn time is the minimum time for a full 360° turn with constant speed and altitude. Given for an altitude of 1000 m.
The maximum rate of climb with a steady-state speed or a speed determined by a climb chart. Given for the aircraft's clean configuration with full internal fuel tanks and at sea level.
Maximum altitude is the highest altitude at which steady level flight at a constant speed is possible with the maximum thrust or engine power
Maximum speed is the maximum speed an aircraft can reach in level flight
The wing loading determines the takeoff and landing speed of an aircraft, its maneuverability, maximum flight distance and other characteristics
Automatic target tracking is a system that automatically tracks the movement of a target after it has been detected and captured, calculating its current coordinates and movement parameters

This mode is strictly for listening to the game with stereo headphones. Important sounds receive more precise horizontal and vertical positioning. These are sounds from ground vehicle engines, aircraft, all weapons, and some explosions. Please note that all third-party game audio enhancement drivers, such as Windows Sonic, Dolby Atmos, DTS Headphone x, and any virtual 5.1/7.1 systems, must be disabled.

In addition to toggling this mode on and off, you can also apply stereo widening to the game audio, while enhanced positioning remains enabled. Stereo widening can provide a more natural and detailed experience from game audio. Choose the preset that best suits your headphones and audio drivers.

Please note, this mode is not compatible with sound mods. Enabling a sound mod will disable it

Play a test sound signal to configure the extended stereo panorama mode. The signal contains three tones of different pitches. Try to select the preset in which all three tones sound as close to the center of the stereo panorama as possible.

The extended stereo panorama mode is very sensitive to the type of headphones and audio card drivers, so some modes may sound biased toward the right or left channel
